original_abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged as highly effective tools for modeling graph-structured data within recommender systems. Despite their success, real-world recommendation environments are often characterized by complex, heterogeneous relationships—such as social influences among users and knowledge-driven dependencies between items—that offer rich semantic cues for refining user preference learning. In this paper, we address the challenges of heterogeneous graph-enhanced relational learning. While contrastive self-supervised learning has recently gained traction in the field, existing methods often fail to fully capture the nuances of multi-relational data. To bridge this gap, we introduce Heterogeneous Graph Contrastive Learning (HGCL), a framework designed to integrate diverse relational semantics into user-item interaction modeling via contrastive-enhanced knowledge transfer across multiple graph views. Recognizing that the impact of side information is inherently subjective, we further augment HGCL with meta-networks. This architectural choice enables a personalized knowledge transformer that facilitates adaptive contrastive augmentation tailored to individual users and items. Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets demonstrate that HGCL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art recommendation paradigms. Detailed ablation studies further confirm the efficacy of our key components in driving performance gains. Our implementation code is publicly available at https://github.com/HKUDS/HGCL.
humanized_abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have been widely adopted as one of the primary ways to model graph-structured data in recommender systems. However, in practice, the underlying recommendation environment is often highly complex and heterogeneous, which contains a variety of social and knowledge-based relationships for users and items, which can provide abundant semantic information for improving user preference learning. Therefore, it is imperative to study the heterogeneous graph-enhanced relational learning. While contrastive self-supervised learning has drawn significant attention in the research community, current solutions for modeling multi-relational graphs are not optimal. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework named Heterogeneous Graph Contrastive Learning (HGCL) that enhances user-item interaction learning by contrastively transferring relational knowledge from multiple graph views for better fusing heterogeneous relational semantics. Moreover, the side information in heterogeneous graphs is often subjective, so we deploy a set of meta-networks to serve as a personalized knowledge transformer to augment the contrastive augmentation process in a learning-to-augmento (LatM) fashion, which adaptively transforms the heterogeneous graphs into optimal contrastive learning objects for each user and item pair. Experiments on three large-scale datasets validate the superiority of HGCL over the state-of-the-art recommendation methods, and ablation studies confirm the contributions of each component. Our implementation code is available at https://github.com/HKUDS/HGCL.
